one_line_takeaway: COLD-Attack recasts LLM jailbreaking as controllable text generation, using energy-based Langevin sampling to produce fluent, low-perplexity, controllable adversarial prompts (suffix, paraphrase, or position-inserted) roughly 10x faster than GCG while evading perplexity filters.
key_finding: COLD-Attack's headline value is stealth + controllability + efficiency, not raw ASR supremacy (the paper explicitly positions itself as a complement, not replacement, to GCG/AutoDAN). Continuation setting (Table 2): ASR 100/96/92/92% and ASR-G 86/84/90/66% on Vicuna/Guanaco/Mistral/Llama2, achieving best or second-best ASR while producing the lowest perplexity among automatic methods (PPL 32.96/30.55/26.24/24.83, beating AutoDAN-Zhu on every model) — GCG ties/beats on raw ASR (100%) but only via an early-termination artifact and yields high-PPL gibberish. Efficiency: ~10x faster than GCG (about 20 min vs 3.23 hrs per request; e.g. 16.43 vs 156.93 min on Vicuna, single V100). Paraphrasing setting (Table 5): ASR 96/98/98/86% vs PRISM 52/58/18/4%, PAWS 56/56/24/6%, and GPT-4-paraphrasing 40/42/10/4% — a large margin. Sentiment steering is model-dependent: negative sentiment raises ASR-G by +30% (Mistral) and +14% (Guanaco), while Llama2 is more vulnerable to positive sentiment (+18%). Position setting (Tables 6/8): ASR and ASR-G both >80%, Succ >68% across all models; lowest PPL, ~2x below AutoDAN-Zhu and ~40x below GCG. Diversity (Table 3): consistently best (e.g. Self-BLEU 0.29-0.48 vs AutoDAN-Zhu's 1.00). Transferability is modest: attacks crafted on open models transfer to GPT-3.5 at ASR-G 16-32% and to GPT-4 at ASR-G 30-36% (vs ~2-6% prompt-only), comparable to AutoDAN-Zhu.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is COLD-Attack, a white-box framework for generating stealthy AND controllable jailbreak prompts against aligned LLMs. Three specific novel contributions: (1) it formally formulates the "controllable attack generation" problem and observes that it is identical to controllable text generation except for one added constraint (that the output must be a successful attack), thereby bridging jailbreaking with the NLP subfield of controllable text generation; (2) it adapts COLD (Energy-based Constrained Decoding with Langevin Dynamics, from the cited Qin et al. 2022) by integrating GCG's adversarial cost (cited Zou et al. 2023) as an extra energy term, producing an energy-based sampler over continuous logit space plus a guided decoding step; (3) it demonstrates three attack settings, two of which are new: the existing fluent-suffix (continuation) setting, plus new "paraphrasing constraint" (rewrite the query into a semantically-equivalent attack, optionally under sentiment steering) and "position constraint" (insert a bridge attack between the query and an output-control prompt) settings. NOTE ON ATTRIBUTION: COLD itself and GCG's cost function are cited prior work, not this paper's inventions; the truncated abstract supplied to earlier reviewers conflated GCG's cost function with the method, which is why full-text grounding matters here.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to a target LLM's gradients/logits (open-source models like Vicuna/Llama-2/Guanaco/Mistral, or via transfer to closed GPT-3.5/GPT-4). Goal: elicit harmful content from safety-aligned LLMs while satisfying stealth/controllability constraints — fluency to evade perplexity-based filters, semantic-preserving paraphrase to hide the attack position (defeating suffix-removal defenses), and context insertion with controlled output style/sentiment/format. The paper evaluates robustness against perplexity filtering, GPT-4 paraphrasing preprocessing, OpenAI moderation, SmoothLLM, Llama Guard, and RAIN (appendix), reporting that semantically-meaningful COLD-Attack prompts largely bypass perplexity filters up to a threshold of 60 and remain more robust than GCG under character-perturbation defenses like SmoothLLM.
limitations: (1) White-box only — requires gradient/logit access; effectiveness on closed models is limited to transfer (ASR-G only 16-36% on GPT-3.5/GPT-4). (2) Does not dominate on raw ASR — GCG reaches 100% on three models (via early-termination), so COLD-Attack's edge is stealth/efficiency/controllability, and the authors frame it as complementary, not superior. (3) Main tables use a 50-instruction AdvBench subset (full 520 relegated to appendix). (4) Substring-match ASR is coarse; the paper mitigates with GPT-4 ASR-G but that inherits GPT-4-judge noise. (5) The main experimental setting omits the system prompt (following Huang et al. 2023); the authors acknowledge GitHub users flagged this and address system-prompt impact only in appendix D.8. (6) Per-request cost (~20 min on a V100) is still non-trivial for large-scale use. (7) Defense-evasion results are largely qualitative/appendix (Figure 6, Table 15) rather than in the main text.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) — 50-instruction curated subset for main experiments; full 520-instruction set reported in appendix Table 20
quantitative_results: Continuation/suffix attack (Table 2): COLD-Attack ASR 100/96/92/92% and ASR-G 86/84/90/66% on Vicuna/Guanaco/Mistral/Llama2; lowest PPL among automatic methods (32.96/30.55/26.24/24.83), beating AutoDAN-Zhu on all four
Efficiency: ~10x faster than GCG/GCG-reg (~20 min vs ~3.23 hrs per request); e.g. Vicuna 16.43 vs GCG 156.93 min, Llama2 15.05 vs 235.25 min on a single V100 (Table 4)
Paraphrasing attack (Table 5): ASR 96/98/98/86% vs PRISM 52/58/18/4%, PAWS 56/56/24/6%, GPT-4-paraphrase 40/42/10/4%; BERTScore ~0.71-0.74 (near GPT-4's 0.75)
Sentiment steering (Table 7): negative sentiment raises ASR-G +30% on Mistral and +14% on Guanaco; Llama2 gains +18% ASR-G under positive vs negative sentiment

Position attack (Tables 6 & 8, Llama2): ASR and ASR-G both >80%, Succ >68% across sentiment/lexical/format/style constraints; COLD-Attack PPL ~2x lower than AutoDAN-Zhu and ~40x lower than GCG (e.g. 59.53 vs 113.27 vs 2587.90 under sentiment control)
Diversity (Table 3): COLD-Attack best on all models, e.g. Self-BLEU 0.48/0.34/0.31/0.29 vs AutoDAN-Zhu 1.00/1.00/1.00/1.00 and DNS 0.79-0.96
Transferability (Tables 13/14): attacks transfer to GPT-3.5-turbo at ASR-G 16-32% and to GPT-4 at ASR-G 30-36% (vs ~2-6% prompt-only), comparable to AutoDAN-Zhu
Perplexity-filter evasion: most COLD-Attack prompts bypass a PPL threshold of 60, considered restrictive since AdvBench malicious queries themselves exceed PPL 100 (Figure 6)
baselines_compared: AutoDAN-Liu (Liu et al. 2023d) — reported for completeness, not treated as a baseline since it partially uses manually-crafted prompts
Paraphrasing baselines: PRISM (Thompson & Post 2020), PAWS (Zhang et al. 2019), GPT-4-based paraphrasing
Defenses evaluated: perplexity filtering, GPT-4 paraphrasing preprocessing, OpenAI Moderation, SmoothLLM, Llama Guard, RAIN
